codeBar.domain: AI-Agent Security-Review Readiness Audit Product (Enterprise Credibility Stack)

bar_criteria.best_in_class: Ed25519 (RFC 8037) with DSSE PAE (RFC 9453), in-toto v1 Statement (RFC 6962/9162 Merkle), no reimplemented crypto primitives, constant-time verification, all tests passing (wave149, wave150, wave161, wave166, wave162)

N+7 three-party model: builder Ed25519 key (src/ed25519.js) + auditor Ed25519 key (src/auditor-attestation.js) + verifier (binder check #22). Keys MUST differ. Signature blocks include key fingerprint cross-check to prevent substitution. Auditor blocks bind artifact_hash, eval_score, k_score, external holdout hashes with cross-validation before embedding.

src/transparency-log.js: RFC 6962/9162 Merkle tree, SHA256 hash chain (tamper-evident), C2SP signed-note checkpoints, inclusion proofs verifiable offline. No external dependency on Rekor (future hook ready). Deterministic canonical JSON serialization.

src/intoto-slsa.js: SLSA Build L2 (shape, not hardened-builder claim). in-toto Statement v1 with SLSA Provenance v1 predicate. Honesty about conformance level (never claims L3). DSSE envelope excluded from artifact_hash. Tested (wave150, wave162, wave146).

src/ed25519.js#loadOrCreateDefaultSigner: stable per-machine key (mode 0o600) with explicit fallback to env vars. src/secrets-vault.js: AES-256-GCM encryption at rest, key derivation from shared secret (no hardcoded master key). Distinct env namespaces: KOLM_ED25519_* vs KOLM_AUDITOR_ED25519_* prevent cross-key confusion.

37 test files in tests/ including wave149 (Ed25519 default), wave150 (sigstore integration), wave166 (auditor attestation), wave162 (in-toto), wave161 (Ed25519 policy). Isolated test environments with fresh keys per test. Node.js native test runner (no external test framework magic).

Minimal: 15 dependencies, no @sigstore/* (reimplemented DSSE + Ed25519 in-tree). node:crypto (built-in), node:fs (built-in), express + helmet (security headers), no transitive bloat. package-lock.json pinned. ESLint configured.

GitHub Actions: test-suite.yml, lint.yml, sbom.yml, publish-pypi.yml, kolm-ci-pipeline.yml (template). Self-guarding: secrets.KOLM_KEY required. Artifact signing: zip + Ed25519 receipt + optional sigstore. No build artifact tampering surface (offline verification only).

In-code: every module (ed25519.js, intoto-receipt.js, auditor-attestation.js, transparency-log.js) has header documenting scope, constraints, compliance standards. Wave-specific test files name the standards (RFC 8037, RFC 6962, RFC 9162, DSSE, in-toto v1). README.md positions as 'AI compiler' not 'security audit product' (MISMATCH).

Code comments explicitly name threat boundaries: HMAC-only (Wave 0) → symmetric, cannot prove to third party. Wave 149 Ed25519 → asymmetric, offline-verifiable. N+7 auditor layer → prevents self-attestation. crossCheckAttestation prevents drift (check #22 branch d). Key fingerprint mismatch rejection (check #22 branch e).

red_flags: CRITICAL POSITIONING MISMATCH: README.md + public/index.html position kolm as 'AI compiler / distillation engine' ('compile your AI workload', 'capture, distill, compile'), NOT as 'AI-agent security-review readiness audit product.' The credibility stack (ed25519.js, auditor-attestation.js, intoto-receipt.js, transparency-log.js) is production-grade but exists to support *artifact verification*, not *auditor attestation*. Landing page must immediately establish the audit product narrative or this credibility stack looks like feature bloat on a mispositioned product.

SECRETS HANDLING: src/secrets-vault.js stores encrypted secrets at ~/.kolm/secrets-vault.json with mode 0o600, but file chmod is wrapped in try/catch with 'deliberate: cleanup' comment—fails silently on Windows/restricted filesystems. No explicit check that file is actually unreadable after write. Could expose API keys/signing keys if chmod fails.

CI/CD GUARD TIMING: kolm-ci-pipeline.yml uses 'if: ${{ secrets.KOLM_KEY != '' }}' — this evaluates at job START, not at GitHub action registration time. If KOLM_KEY is rotated mid-pipeline, a long-running job may use a revoked key without notification.

AUDITOR KEY ISOLATION INCOMPLETE: loadAuditorKeyFromEnv() and loadAuditorKeyFromFile() create fresh crypto.createPrivateKey() objects with no validation of key type (could accept RSA, ECDSA, etc., not just Ed25519). Should explicitly validate crypto.createPrivateKey().asymmetricKeyType === 'ed25519'.

TRANSPARENCY LOG NO EXTERNAL ANCHORING: src/transparency-log.js implements RFC 6962/9162 Merkle tree + checkpoint notes, but has no integration to Rekor, Sigstore, or a public ledger. Checkpoint signature is Ed25519 over C2SP note, but the log itself is in-process memory or a single-machine store. Without distributed ledger anchor, 'append-only' is only as durable as a single database/file.

FINGERPRINT COLLISION RISK (LOW): keyFingerprint() returns first 32 hex chars (128 bits) of SHA256(SPKI DER). Industry standard is 160+ bits for collision resistance at high scale. Current design is pragmatic but susceptible to birthday attacks if used for >2^64 keys across many auditors.

VERIFYINCLUSIONPROOF MISSING: src/transparency-log.js#inclusionProof() emits proof but no public verifyInclusionProof() export for offline verification. Users cannot independently verify inclusion without calling into the verifyTransparencyAppend logic (which is specific to append validation, not general proof verification).

AUDITOR ATTESTATION DRIFT TOLERANCE: crossCheckAttestation allows k_score.composite drift up to 1e-4 (0.0001). This is documented but aggressive for regulatory contexts—different rounding between auditor observation and re-check could pass silently if composite differs by 0.00009 but was rounded differently on display.

NO HSMS/SIGNING SERVICE INTEGRATION: Builder + auditor keys are both assumed to be PEM files or env vars. No support for cloud signing (AWS Signing, Azure Key Vault, GCP KMS), Yubikey, TPM, or Nitro Enclave attestation. Enterprise deployments will fork to add HSM paths; code review will miss custom signing paths.

INTOTO_RECEIPT_VERSION HARDCODED: src/intoto-receipt.js exports INTOTO_RECEIPT_VERSION = 'w921-intoto-receipt-v1' but there is no version negotiation or backward-compatibility layer. If a future update changes statement shape or adds optional fields, old verifiers may reject new receipts or miss new fields silently.

CROSS-TENANT LEAK RISK IN TRANSPARENCY LOG: TransparencyLog constructor accepts tenant_id but appends occur via _rows() which filters by tenant_id. However, if store.find() returns unsorted rows or if _rows() is called with different tenant contexts in concurrent code, tenant boundary could blur. No explicit tenancy test in wave-suite.

BINDER REQUIRES SHARED SECRET FOR SIGNATURE VERIFICATION: src/binder.js requires RECIPE_RECEIPT_SECRET to verify HMAC over receipt chain. If a customer wants to verify an artifact offline without the secret (common for enterprise scenarios), they cannot verify chain integrity—only structure. Documented ('unverified' state) but limits audit independence.

NO SIGNED RECEIPT ROTATION POLICY: receipt.signature_ed25519 is embedded in the artifact itself. If a signing key is ever compromised, old artifacts cannot be invalidated or re-signed—they carry the bad key forever. No revocation list or CRL equivalent.

INTOTO DSSE PAE BYTE-LENGTH BUG RISK: pae() function constructs PAE correctly but there is no explicit test asserting byte-length correctness (the comment references the spec example but tests do not assert the exact PAE bytes). A single character-vs-byte confusion bug would break cosign interop silently.

TEST ISOLATION GOOD BUT NO CONCURRENT TEST SAFETY: tests use fs.mkdtempSync but tests run with --test-concurrency=1 in package.json. If developers run tests in parallel (npm test:parallel), tests may collide on shared resources (e.g., KOLM_ED25519_KEY_STORE if env setup is inherited).

MISSING FORMAL SPECIFICATION DOCUMENT: Modules have excellent inline comments, but there is no formal specification (RFC/spec document) published for kolm's signing/attestation model. For enterprise auditors, 'formal spec' is often a required artifact.

SEKRETSCHRIFT RISK: src/ed25519.js, auditor-attestation.js, transparency-log.js are not reviewed by a cryptographer external to the project. Code review only covers 'does it match the RFC' not 'does the RFC cover all threats'.

code_audit.area: AI-Agent Security-Review Readiness Audit Product: Attestation & Signing Stack + Enterprise Website Positioning
code_audit.summary: The attestation/signing stack (ed25519.js, intoto-receipt.js, auditor-attestation.js, transparency-log.js, transparency-anchor.js) is state-of-the-art in cryptographic correctness: RFC 8037 Ed25519, DSSE PAE compliance, RFC 6962 Merkle trees, in-toto v1 Statement conformance, and N+7 third-party auditor architecture are all correctly implemented and tested. Key rotation (keys.js), secrets management (secrets-vault.js with AES-256-GCM), and enterprise safeguards (file mode 0o600, per-tenant isolation) are production-grade. However, THREE CRITICAL GAPS threaten enterprise credibility: (1) Website positioning contradicts the product pivot—homepage still says "AI control plane for teams" when the credibility stack is built for "security-audit platform signed by elite researchers"—destroying trust on first impression; (2) Researcher/auditor credibility network is completely invisible (no bios, no co-signing terms, no "world-class" proof), despite Wave 166 N+7 auditor-attestation infrastructure being ready; (3) Auditor key type validation is missing—loadAuditorKeyFromEnv/loadAuditorKeyFromFile accept any PEM type (RSA, ECDSA, EdDSA) without explicit asymmetricKeyType check, allowing non-Ed25519 keys to silently pass. Four medium-severity bugs surface: file chmod silent failure (Windows/restricted FS risk), transparency log no external ledger anchor (append-only only as durable as single process), incomplete DSSE PAE byte-length test coverage (no explicit assertion on PAE byte count), and no offline verifyInclusionProof export (users must call verifyTransparencyAppend, not general-purpose proof verification). Grade reflects: A-tier crypto + full test coverage (37 test files, wave166/149/161 passing) + RFC compliance, versus missing positioning clarity and researcher visibility that would block enterprise sales.
strengths: Ed25519 signing is RFC 8037 compliant, uses Node.js native crypto.sign/verify, avoids reimplemented primitives
DSSE PAE (Pre-Authentication Encoding) correctly implements RFC 9453: byte-length encoding, standard-base64 signature wrapping, tested against cosign interop (wave921-intoto-attestation.test.js)
in-toto v1 Statement shape is correct: _type, subject array with non-empty sha256 digests, predicateType, predicate
SLSA Provenance v1 integration is honest: declared Build L2 (not L3), never claims hardened-builder properties
N+7 three-party auditor architecture is solid: distinct builder Ed25519 key vs. auditor Ed25519 key, fingerprint cross-check prevents substitution (auditor-attestation.js lines 277-286)
Transparent key custody: loadOrCreateDefaultSigner caches per-machine signing key at ~/.kolm/signing-key.pem with mode 0o600, preference for explicit KOLM_ED25519_PRIVATE_KEY or _PATH in CI

Auditor attestation block includes binding fields: artifact_hash (hex64), eval_score, k_score, external_holdout_hash, tenant_shadow_corpus_hash; crossCheckAttestation re-validates all signed claims (auditor-attestation.js lines 319-411)
Transparency log implements RFC 6962/9162 Merkle tree correctly: leafHash, computeRoot, inclusionPath, verifyInclusion from merkle.js, tamper-evident hash chain (sha256(prev_hash || body))
Merkle batch anchoring (transparency-anchor.js): O(log n) inclusion proofs per receipt, receipt anchor block stored outside signed body so Ed25519 receipt signature stays valid

Secrets vault uses AES-256-GCM at rest with key derivation from shared secret (secrets-vault.js), not hardcoded master key; distinct env namespaces KOLM_ED25519_* vs KOLM_AUDITOR_ED25519_* prevent cross-key confusion

Test coverage is comprehensive: 37 test files, isolated environments (per-test key stores, fresh ULID receipt IDs), wave149/150/161/166 passing, no external test-framework magic (Node.js native test runner)

Key rotation lifecycle (keys.js): rotateKey generates fresh Ed25519 pair, writes to disk (mode 0o600) for local, returns PEM in-memory for hosted KMS, 30-day overlap default (NIST SP 800-57 Part 1 Rev 5 aligned)

Receipt builder (gateway-receipt.js) is deterministic: no wall-clock reads outside explicit timestamp param, canonicalForSigning excludes signature blocks, HMAC + Ed25519 layering preserves backward compatibility

Conformance strings are explicit: ed25519.js 'kolm-ed25519-v1', intoto-receipt.js 'in-toto Statement v1 + ITE-6 inference predicate (Ed25519 key-custody attestation; not proof-of-compute)', never overstates (no L3, no hardware-proof)
Binder check #22 validates auditor != builder via fingerprint comparison, preventing self-attestation fraud
Public-key JWK export (ed25519.js publicKeyJwk) follows RFC 8037 OKP format for JWKS endpoint / /.well-known/jwks.json interop
CI/CD self-guarding: GitHub Actions test-suite.yml, lint.yml, sbom.yml require secrets.KOLM_KEY for artifact signing
Halborn pentest result (2026-04) shows strong security posture: 0 Critical, 1 High, 3 Medium, 3 Info findings; retest closed 2026-05-06
Package.json has only 15 dependencies, no @sigstore/*, no heavy transitive trees; ed25519 + DSSE + Merkle all in-tree
Offline verifiability works for core operations: Ed25519 verify (public key only), Merkle inclusion proofs, hash-chain checks require no kolm secret

Update homepage nav: add 'Auditors' link to /researchers page. Add 'Independently verified by [Researcher Name], [Credential]' badge to hero or 'Proof' page, linking to Halborn report.
gaps.effort: 8 hours (write bios, create /researchers page, add badge/CTA flow)

gaps.issue: CRITICAL: Auditor key type validation missing—non-Ed25519 keys silently pass
gaps.evidence: auditor-attestation.js lines 122-127 (loadAuditorKeyFromEnv): 'const keyObj = crypto.createPrivateKey(pem); publicKey = crypto.createPublicKey(keyObj).export(...)' does NOT validate keyObj.asymmetricKeyType === 'ed25519'. crypto.createPrivateKey accepts RSA, ECDSA, EdDSA, DSA. If an operator passes an RSA or ECDSA key via KOLM_AUDITOR_ED25519_PRIVATE_KEY, it is accepted without error. Later code assumes Ed25519 (e.g., ed25519Sign uses null algorithm param), so an RSA key would fail at sign time (ed25519Sign line 76), but validation failure happens late—after the key is already loaded and treated as valid. loadAuditorKeyFromFile (lines 149-154) has the same gap.
gaps.fix: Add explicit type check in both functions. After 'const keyObj = crypto.createPrivateKey(pem):', insert: 'if (keyObj.asymmetricKeyType !== "ed25519") { throw new Error(`auditor-attestation: invalid key type "${keyObj.asymmetricKeyType}", expected "ed25519"`); }'. Apply same check in src/ed25519.js loadSignerKeyFromEnv (line 137) for consistency. Add test case: loadAuditorKeyFromEnv with RSA key → throws with clear message.
gaps.effort: 1 hour (add 2-line validation, write 1 test)

gaps.issue: File chmod silent failure: secrets-vault.js and ed25519.js wrap chmod in try/catch with 'deliberate: cleanup' comment—fails silently on Windows/restricted FS
gaps.evidence: secrets-vault.js lines 49, 71: 'try { fs.chmodSync(p, 0o600); } catch {} // deliberate: cleanup'. ed25519.js line 223: fs.writeFileSync(..., {mode: 0o600}) passes mode, but mkdir (line 217) also passes mode. When chmod fails on Windows (Windows does not support POSIX file modes) or restricted filesystems, the catch silently swallows the error. A signing key written without mode 0o600 is world-readable, leaking the private key if /tmp or ~/.kolm is accessible to other users. For a 'world-class' security product, this is footgun territory.
gaps.fix: Replace silent catch with logging + explicit failure only on non-Windows: 'if (process.platform !== 'win32') { try { fs.chmodSync(p, 0o600); } catch (e) { console.warn(`WARNING: chmod ${p} failed: ${e.message}`); } }'. Or: validate chmod succeeded: 'fs.chmodSync(p, 0o600); const stat = fs.statSync(p); if ((stat.mode & 0o077) !== 0) { throw new Error(`chmod ${p} to 0o600 failed: mode is ${(stat.mode & 0o777).toString(8)}`); }'. Document in README: 'On Windows, key files are stored in the user's home directory; rely on OS ACLs to prevent unauthorized access.'
gaps.effort: 2 hours (add validation, test on Windows, update docs)

gaps.issue: Transparency log no external ledger anchor—append-only guarantee is only as durable as single machine/process memory
gaps.evidence: transparency-log.js (lines 1-35) implements RFC 6962/9162 Merkle tree + SHA256 hash chain + Ed25519 checkpoint signatures, mirrors Sigstore Rekor witness model. However, the log is in-process memory (this._mem) or a single-machine store (opts.store). There is no integration to Rekor, no public transparency endpoint, no /prod/transparency/v1/entries API. Without distributed ledger anchor, 'append-only' is only as durable as a single database/file: if kolm's process crashes or the database is wiped, historical entries vanish. Sigstore Rekor is public (rekor.sigstore.dev); a serious audit product needs public verifiable logs. Checkpoint notes are signed (signTreeHead), but the signature is only as valuable as the log it commits to.
gaps.fix: Phase 1 (current): document the limitation in /docs/transparency-log: 'The local transparency log is append-only within a single kolm instance. For enterprise deployments requiring durability across restarts, operators may integrate with Rekor (rekor.sigstore.dev) via the submitFn hook (transparency-anchor.js anchorBatch line 175).' Phase 2 (roadmap): expose POST /prod/transparency/v1/entries and GET /prod/transparency/v1/entries/{seq} so the log is queryable; periodically submit batch roots to Rekor or a custom ledger. For MVP: add statement to /security/posture.html: 'Transparency log is append-only within the current process; restarting kolm or wiping the database clears historical entries. For compliance deployments, we integrate with Sigstore Rekor (submitted Q3 2026).'
gaps.effort: Phase 1: 2 hours (docs + /security update). Phase 2 (roadmap): 20 hours (Rekor integration, API endpoints, tests)

gaps.issue: DSSE PAE byte-length correctness test missing—no explicit assertion that PAE bytes are correct byte count
gaps.evidence: wave921-intoto-attestation.test.js line 181 ('the DSSE signature is computed over PAE bytes') calls pae(env.payloadType, payloadBytes) and verifies the signature, but does NOT assert the exact byte count of the PAE output. The DSSE spec example (intoto-slsa.js lines 63-66) shows: 'PAE("http://example.com/HelloWorld", "hello world") = "DSSEv1 29 http://example.com/HelloWorld 11 hello world"' — this is 61 bytes total. A single off-by-one in the byte-length encoding or a char-vs-byte confusion would produce a different PAE, breaking cosign interop, but the test only verifies the signature round-trips, not the PAE bytes themselves. This is the #1 interop trap per code comment (intoto-slsa.js line 62).
gaps.fix: Add test assertion in wave921-intoto-attestation.test.js after line 186: 'const expectedPaePrefix = Buffer.from("DSSEv1 ", "utf8"); assert(paeBytes.slice(0, 7).equals(expectedPaePrefix), "PAE does not start with DSSEv1 "); // Validate the payloadType length encoding is decimal, no leading zeros. const parts = paeBytes.toString("utf8").split(" "); assert.equal(parts[0], "DSSEv1"); assert.equal(parseInt(parts[1], 10), payloadBytes.length, "payload length encoding mismatch");' Also add a concrete vector test: 'test("pae produces the exact DSSE spec example", () => { const paeBytes = pae("http://example.com/HelloWorld", "hello world"); const expected = Buffer.from("DSSEv1 29 http://example.com/HelloWorld 11 hello world", "utf8"); assert(paeBytes.equals(expected)); });'
gaps.effort: 1 hour (add 2 test assertions)

gaps.issue: Transparency log no public offline verifyInclusionProof export—users must call verifyTransparencyAppend, not general-purpose proof verification
gaps.evidence: transparency-log.js#inclusionProof (lines 188-206) returns { ok, leaf_hash, leaf_index, tree_size, audit_path, root_hash }. The code calls verifyInclusion (from merkle.js) internally in verifyTransparencyAppend (lines 370-376), but there is NO exported function 'verifyInclusionProof()' for offline users. A researcher downloading a transparency checkpoint + an inclusion proof must inline merkle.verifyInclusion, not call a single function. This is a DX gap: researchers expect to call log.verifyInclusionProof({proof, entry}) and get { ok, reason }. Sigstore Rekor exports verify-inclusion primitives directly.
gaps.fix: Export a verifyInclusionProof function at the module level: 'export function verifyInclusionProof({ proof, pinnedRoot }) { if (!proof || !proof.audit_path) return { ok: false, reason: "no proof" }; const inc = verifyInclusion({ leafHash: proof.leaf_hash, leafIndex: proof.leaf_index, treeSize: proof.tree_size, inclusionPath: proof.audit_path, root: pinnedRoot || proof.root_hash }); return { ok: inc.ok, reason: inc.reason }; }' Update /docs/transparency-log to show: 'Offline verification: import { verifyInclusionProof } from "./transparency-log.js"; const result = verifyInclusionProof({ proof, pinnedRoot }); console.log(result.ok);'
gaps.effort: 1 hour (export 1 function, add 1 doc example)

gaps.issue: Attestation block post-build signature validation is present but drift tolerance for k_score.composite is aggressive (1e-4 = 0.0001)
gaps.evidence: auditor-attestation.js lines 362-373 (crossCheckAttestation for k_score): 'const drift = Math.abs(sComp - mComp); if (drift > 1e-4) { return { ok: false, reason: ... } }'. This allows 0.0001 difference. In regulatory contexts (SOC 2, HIPAA audit), different rounding between auditor display (e.g., 0.9234 displayed as 92.34%) and re-check computation (0.9233556 recomputed, rounded to 0.9234) could silently pass when the true values differ. The tolerance is documented (header comment lines 317-318), but named 'drift > 1e-4' suggests the real danger: floating-point noise from independent re-computation. For compliance: recommend 1e-6 (six decimal places = 0.0000001) or exact match with explicit rounding agreed pre-audit.
gaps.fix: Document the drift tolerance explicitly in auditor-attestation.js header: '// k_score.composite drift tolerance: 1e-4. This is pragmatic for floating-point noise but aggressive for regulatory contexts. Consider 1e-6 for compliance-sensitive audits. Auditor and builder should agree on rounding (e.g., "round composite to 4 decimal places") before attestation.' Add a parameter to crossCheckAttestation for custom tolerance: 'export function crossCheckAttestation(block, manifest, opts = {}) { const drift_tolerance = opts.k_score_drift_tolerance || 1e-4; ...'. Update tests to verify both tight (1e-6) and loose (1e-4) scenarios.
gaps.effort: 2 hours (add parameter, update tests, document)

gaps.issue: No HSMS/Signing Service integration—Builder and auditor keys assumed to be PEM files or env vars; enterprise will fork custom signing paths
gaps.evidence: ed25519.js and auditor-attestation.js assume signing keys are PEM strings (loadOrCreateDefaultSigner, loadAuditorKeyFromEnv, loadAuditorKeyFromFile). Enterprise security teams use AWS Signing, Azure Key Vault, GCP KMS, Yubikey, TPM, or Nitro Enclave attestation. A team needing HSM support will fork, wrap the signing path in a custom 'sign via KMS' function, and code review will miss custom signing paths that bypass the Ed25519 module. Enterprise bar: design for HSM integration at the interface level (even if the implementation is future-scope).
gaps.fix: Add a 'SigningService' interface abstraction in ed25519.js: 'export class SigningService { sign(data) { throw new Error("sign() not implemented"); } getPublicKey() { throw new Error("getPublicKey() not implemented"); } }' and export a 'PemSigningService' implementation that uses PEM. Document in /docs/hsm-integration.md: 'Custom signing services: create a SigningService subclass, pass it to buildSignatureBlock via opts.signer. Example: AWS KMS signer coming Q3 2026.' Add interface/README comment: 'To add HSM support: extend SigningService, override sign() to call your KMS API, override getPublicKey() to return the public key from your KMS. This keeps custom signing paths within the module boundary.'
gaps.effort: 4 hours (design interface, document, add 1-2 example stubs)

gaps.issue: INTOTO_RECEIPT_VERSION hardcoded as 'w921-intoto-receipt-v1'—no version negotiation or backward-compatibility layer
gaps.evidence: intoto-receipt.js line 56: 'export const INTOTO_RECEIPT_VERSION = "w921-intoto-receipt-v1"'. If a future update changes the Statement shape or adds optional fields, old verifiers may reject new receipts or miss new fields silently. The constant is embedded in every receipt (intoto-receipt.js line 275 in signInTotoBundle return), but verifyInTotoBundle (line 410) does NOT re-check the version against a minimum supported version.
gaps.fix: Add version negotiation logic to verifyInTotoBundle: 'export const SUPPORTED_INTOTO_RECEIPT_VERSIONS = ["w921-intoto-receipt-v1"]; ... const versionOk = SUPPORTED_INTOTO_RECEIPT_VERSIONS.includes(bundleOrEnvelope.version || "w921-intoto-receipt-v1"); if (!versionOk) { return { ok: false, reason: `unsupported receipt version: ${bundleOrEnvelope.version}` }; }'. Document in /docs/versioning.md: 'Receipt version is embedded in every signed bundle. We commit to backward compatibility: v1 receipts remain verifiable in future versions. New versions will be explicitly enumerated in SUPPORTED_INTOTO_RECEIPT_VERSIONS.'
gaps.effort: 2 hours (add version check, test backward compat, document)

gaps.issue: Transparency log no explicit tenancy test—if store.find() returns unsorted rows or _rows() is called with different tenant contexts in concurrent code, tenant boundary could blur
gaps.evidence: transparency-log.js constructor (line 106) accepts tenant_id but appends occur via _rows() (lines 113-123) which filters by tenant_id. However, _rows() calls store.find() (line 115) with a filter callback that assumes find() returns unsorted rows, then sorts them (line 117). If a custom store implementation returns pre-sorted rows in an unexpected order or if _rows() is called concurrently with different tenant contexts, the sort order could drift. No test explicitly verifies: (1) two different tenant_ids produce isolated log views, (2) interleaving appends from different tenants preserves per-tenant append-only order, (3) concurrent append calls are safe.
gaps.fix: Add test case in wave-suite (e.g., wave921-govern-crypto.test.js): 'test("transparency-log: two tenants have isolated views", () => { const log1 = new TransparencyLog({ tenant_id: "t1", store }); const log2 = new TransparencyLog({ tenant_id: "t2", store }); log1.append("event", {type: "test"}); log2.append("event", {type: "other"}); assert.equal(log1.size(), 1); assert.equal(log2.size(), 1); const rows1 = log1._rows(); const rows2 = log2._rows(); assert(rows1[0].tenant_id === "t1"); assert(rows2[0].tenant_id === "t2"); });' Also test concurrent appends: spawn two async tasks appending to the same tenant, verify seq is strictly monotonic.
gaps.effort: 2 hours (write 2-3 concurrency tests)

gaps.issue: Binder requires RECIPE_RECEIPT_SECRET for HMAC signature verification—customers wanting offline audit cannot verify chain integrity without the secret
gaps.evidence: gateway-receipt.js and binder.js use HMAC over the receipt body. The HMAC is computed/verified with RECIPE_RECEIPT_SECRET. Wave 149/161 added Ed25519 on top (asymmetric, public-key verifiable), so the HMAC is now legacy. However, binder check #X (signature verification) still includes the HMAC block, and verifyReceipt (gateway-receipt.js line 173-185) does NOT expose an 'unverified' or 'hmac-only' state. A customer wanting to verify a receipt offline for an audit cannot do so without the RECIPE_RECEIPT_SECRET—they can only verify the Ed25519 block (asymmetric, no secret needed), not the HMAC. Documented ('unverified' state) in context, but limits audit independence.
gaps.fix: Add an 'hmac_unverified' flag to verifyReceipt return: '{ ok: true, ed25519_verified: true, hmac_verified: false, reason: "Ed25519 signature verified; HMAC requires RECIPE_RECEIPT_SECRET" }'. Update docs: 'Offline receipt verification: Ed25519 signature verification is fully offline (no secret required). HMAC verification (legacy) requires RECIPE_RECEIPT_SECRET and is not part of the public audit chain.'
gaps.effort: 2 hours (update verifyReceipt return shape, add test, update docs)

gaps.issue: No signed receipt rotation policy—receipt.signature_ed25519 embedded in artifact itself; if signing key is compromised, old artifacts carry the bad key forever
gaps.evidence: ed25519.js buildSignatureBlock (lines 262-278) embeds the signature in the receipt. If a signing key is ever compromised, old receipts cannot be invalidated or re-signed—they carry the bad key forever. No revocation list (CRL) or signed-note update mechanism. For a 'world-class' audit product, key compromise is existential: all prior attestations are retroactively untrustworthy. Certificates have CRLs and OCSP; transparency logs have signed-note updates; kolm has no recovery path.
gaps.fix: Phase 1: document the risk in /docs/key-compromise.md: 'If a signing key is compromised: (1) Rotate to a new key immediately (kolm keys rotate). (2) All receipts signed with the old key remain verifiable but are retroactively untrustworthy. (3) Recommend customers re-audit any artifacts verified with the compromised key. We maintain a Compromised Key List (CKL) at /security/compromised-keys.json (signed by a separate authority key, updated within 1 hour of compromise detection).' Phase 2 (roadmap): implement CKL + signed updates, integrate with verifyReceipt. For MVP: explicit crisis communication plan.
gaps.effort: Phase 1: 3 hours (write docs, CKL schema, crisis plan). Phase 2: 12 hours (implement CKL verification, update verifyReceipt)

gaps.issue: No formal specification document for kolm's signing/attestation model—enterprises require RFC/spec artifact for compliance
gaps.evidence: Modules (ed25519.js, intoto-receipt.js, auditor-attestation.js, transparency-log.js) have excellent inline comments and wave-specific tests, but there is no published formal specification (RFC, spec document, or OpenAPI schema) defining the kolm attestation model. Enterprise auditors will ask: 'Where is the specification? Who reviews it? Is it published?' For compliance: a formal spec is required. Sigstore publishes specs; Signers in major frameworks publish RFCs.
gaps.fix: Create /docs/formal-spec.md (or publish as an RFC-style document): (1) Architecture overview (builder key, auditor key, N+7 model). (2) Wire format: receipt schema (JSON), signature_ed25519 block, in-toto Statement, DSSE envelope. (3) Conformance: RFC 8037 (Ed25519), RFC 6962 (Merkle tree), RFC 9453 (DSSE PAE), in-toto v1. (4) Threat model: HMAC-only symmetric (Wave 0) vs. Ed25519 asymmetric (Wave 149+). (5) Auditor attestation block schema. Publish at https://kolm.ai/docs/formal-spec or https://github.com/kolm-ai/specs/. Link from /security and /docs.
gaps.effort: 6 hours (write spec, review for accuracy, publish)

gaps.issue: Code review only internal—ed25519.js, auditor-attestation.js, transparency-log.js never reviewed by external cryptographer
gaps.evidence: Modules match RFCs well (RFC 8037 for Ed25519, RFC 6962/9162 for Merkle, RFC 9453 for DSSE PAE), tests are comprehensive, but code review only covers 'does it match the RFC'—not 'does the RFC cover all threats' or 'are there subtle implementation bugs?' A cryptographer external to the project should review for: constant-time verification (does ed25519.verify avoid timing leaks?), key derivation, entropy sources, any security-critical invariants. This is a credential gap for 'world-class' positioning.
gaps.fix: Engage a third-party cryptography reviewer (e.g., Trail of Bits, Halborn crypto team, academic cryptographer) to conduct a formal review of ed25519.js, auditor-attestation.js, and transparency-log.js against NIST standards, looking for implementation footguns, timing leaks, or RFC misinterpretations. Publish the review (redacted, if needed) at /security/crypto-review-[date].html. Add to homepage or /security: 'Cryptographic core independently reviewed by [Firm] on [Date].' This blocks enterprise deals until done.
gaps.effort: 16-24 hours (engage reviewer, 3-4 week turnaround, publish results)

gaps.issue: Halborn pentest result is hidden—marked noindex, not prominently promoted; strong security posture (0C/1H/3M) goes unmarketed
gaps.evidence: Halborn report (public/security/halborn-2026-04.html) has 0 Critical, 1 High, 3 Medium, 3 Info findings (retest closed 2026-05-06)—a strong result. However, it is buried at /security/halborn-2026-04 with likely noindex header. No press release, no G2 ratings, no '% of Fortune 500' claims. Comparable platforms (Lakera, Noma) promote pentest results on homepage. Enterprise buyers want: 'Independently verified by [Firm] on [Date]' in hero or on first page.
gaps.fix: Add prominent badge to homepage hero or 'Proof' page: 'Independently verified by Halborn (2026-04): 0 Critical, 1 High, 3 Medium findings, retest passed.' Link to /security/halborn-2026-04 (remove noindex). Add to footer or /security: 'Red-team testing completed Q2 2026 by Halborn Security. Full report available on request.' Create a 'Proof' or 'Trust' page showing: penetration test summary, SOC 2 Type II readiness, ISO 27001 scope, red-teaming certifications, quantified credibility (N reports audited, zero false positives, etc.).
gaps.effort: 3 hours (add badge, update SEO, create /proof page template)

code_audit.area: Security Hygiene Review (Secrets, Auth, Input Validation, Injection Surfaces, Dependencies, PII Redaction, Crypto Primitives)
code_audit.summary: The cryptographic attestation/signing core (ed25519.js, auditor-attestation.js, intoto-receipt.js, transparency-log.js) is architecturally sound and uses standard primitives correctly (Ed25519 RFC 8037, DSSE PAE, RFC 6962/9162 Merkle trees). Auth and API key handling follow constant-time comparison discipline. However, critical gaps emerge in: (1) CRITICAL POSITIONING MISMATCH—the live site positions kolm as 'The AI control plane for teams' while the product is pivoting to 'security-audit platform signed by elite researchers' (hero section, meta descriptions, and footer contradict the new direction); (2) SECRETS HANDLING—the secrets-vault.js fs.chmodSync for file permissions is wrapped in try/catch with no validation that chmod actually succeeded, risking credentials exposure on Windows/restricted filesystems; (3) AUDITOR KEY TYPE VALIDATION—loadAuditorKeyFromEnv/loadAuditorKeyFromFile accept PEM without validating crypto.createPrivateKey().asymmetricKeyType === 'ed25519' (could accept RSA/ECDSA); (4) TRANSPARENCY LOG VERIFIER EXPORT—inclusionProof() is a public method but the paired offline verification (verifyInclusion from merkle.js) is not re-exported from transparency-log.js, forcing users to call verifyTransparencyAppend for every inclusion check instead of independent proof verification; (5) FINGERPRINT COLLISION RISK—keyFingerprint() returns 128 bits (first 32 hex chars of SHA256), below 160+ bit recommendation for high-scale auditor identity binding; (6) BINDER REQUIRES SHARED SECRET FOR OFFLINE VERIFICATION—offline verification of receipt chain integrity without RECIPE_RECEIPT_SECRET is impossible (Ed25519 works, but HMAC chain verification requires the secret); (7) K_SCORE DRIFT TOLERANCE—crossCheckAttestation tolerates 1e-4 composite drift, which is aggressive for regulatory contexts where rounding can silently pass differences. No critical code-execution flaws detected (eval, child_process, unsanitized fs, path traversal, SSRF all correctly avoided). Dependencies minimal and pinned. PII redaction (pii-redactor.js, phi-redactor.js) delegates correctly. CI/CD secret timing issue (KOLM_KEY evaluated at job start, not registration time) is low-risk for short-lived workflows but should be documented.
strengths: Crypto primitives correct: Ed25519 signing uses Node.js native crypto, RFC 8037 OKP JWK export, constant-time verification, no reimplemented primitives
Attestation architecture sound: N+7 three-party model enforces distinct builder/auditor keys, fingerprint cross-check prevents key substitution
Constant-time API key comparison via crypto.timingSafeEqual in auth.js#constantTimeEqual
Transparency log implements RFC 6962/9162 Merkle tree with hash-chain tamper detection, checkpoint notes (C2SP style)
DSSE PAE correct per RFC 9453, verified in wave921-intoto-attestation.test.js with cross-tooling interop test
Secrets vault uses AES-256-GCM at rest, key derivation from shared secret (not hardcoded)
PII redaction cleanly delegates to phi-redactor.js, redact/block/detect modes properly enforced
No eval(), child_process, or dynamic code execution detected; verifier.js flags eval as forbidden
API auth middleware properly gates public vs. protected routes, soft-auth for PUBLIC_API preserves tenant_record when key present
Ed25519 default signer (Wave 149) creates stable per-machine key in ~/.kolm/signing-key.pem with mode 0o600, fallback to env vars
Input validation on email (regex /^[^@\s]+@[^@\s]+\.[^@\s]+$/), tenant names (safe-char replacements in auth.js), and IDs (SAFE_ID regex)
Dependencies minimal (15 deps), no @sigstore/* bloat, package-lock.json pinned
Audit chain HMAC verification correctly strips signature blocks before recomputing (Wave 481 fallback logic)
No query-param API key leakage: explicitly rejects ?api_key= with 401 + hint to use headers (auth.js#authMiddleware)

gaps.issue: CRITICAL: Website positioning contradicts product pivot—hero section, meta descriptions, and footer still say 'The AI control plane for teams' (public/index.html line 498, 627) while product is now 'security-audit platform signed by elite researchers.' Enterprise auditors arriving at kolm.ai see control-plane messaging, not audit-credibility narrative. Competitors (Lakera, Noma) lead with researcher bios and 'independent verification.'
gaps.evidence: public/index.html:494-627, lines 6-16, 627; README.md line 4 ('The AI compiler'); meta description cites 'distill frontier-model quality' not 'signed audit evidence'; /security page exists but not linked prominently on homepage
gaps.fix: Rewrite hero section to: 'AI Security Audits. Signed by world-class independent researchers.' Update meta descriptions, nav CTAs, and footer to emphasize 'third-party audit credibility' over 'control plane.' Add /researchers or /auditors page with named expert bios, affiliations, and co-signing terms. Promote Halborn pentest report from /security/halborn-2026-04 (currently noindex) to homepage 'Proof' section with 'Independently verified' badge.
gaps.effort: Medium (HTML/messaging refresh, ~4 pages + nav structure)

gaps.issue: CRITICAL: secrets-vault.js fs.chmodSync(path, 0o600) wrapped in try/catch with no validation—chmod failures fail silently, leaving secrets-vault.json or secrets-vault.key world-readable on Windows or restricted filesystems (line 49, 71).
gaps.evidence: src/secrets-vault.js:49 'try { fs.chmodSync(p, 0o600); } catch {} // deliberate: cleanup' and line 71. No subsequent check that file is actually unreadable. On Windows, chmod is often a no-op; file mode is set at creation time. Fallback: file created with fs.writeFileSync but default umask might expose it.
gaps.fix: After writeFileSync, verify chmod succeeded: `try { fs.chmodSync(p, 0o600); } catch (e) { throw new Error(`failed to set permissions on ${p}: ${e.message}`); }` to surface chmod failures. OR: check file mode post-write via fs.statSync(p).mode, retry if incorrect. For Windows, document that secrets-vault.json must be placed in a directory with ACLs restricting access (e.g., %APPDATA%\kolm with user-only perms), OR use Windows-aware mode setting (WinAPI via ffi if needed).
gaps.effort: Low (exception handling + validation logic, <30 lines)

gaps.issue: HIGH: Auditor key loading does not validate Ed25519 type—loadAuditorKeyFromEnv() and loadAuditorKeyFromFile() call crypto.createPrivateKey(pem) and derive public key, but never check crypto.createPrivateKey().asymmetricKeyType === 'ed25519'. Accepts RSA, ECDSA, or other EC curves silently (lines 123, 150).
gaps.evidence: src/auditor-attestation.js:123, 150; no asymmetricKeyType validation. Caller assumes PEM is Ed25519, but Node.js crypto.createPrivateKey() accepts any valid PEM. Tests (wave166-auditor-attestation.test.js) do not test RSA/ECDSA rejection.
gaps.fix: Add validation after crypto.createPrivateKey(pem): `if (keyObj.asymmetricKeyType !== 'ed25519') { throw new Error(`auditor key must be Ed25519 (got ${keyObj.asymmetricKeyType})`); }` in both loadAuditorKeyFromEnv and loadAuditorKeyFromFile. Add test: try loading a valid RSA PEM, assert it throws.
gaps.effort: Low (2 validation checks + 1 test, <20 lines)

gaps.issue: HIGH: verifyInclusion() function (merkle.js#148) is not re-exported from transparency-log.js—users calling inclusionProof() get the proof object, but cannot independently verify it offline without accessing merkle.js directly or calling verifyTransparencyAppend (which is append-specific, not general proof verification).
gaps.evidence: src/transparency-log.js exports: canonicalEntryJson, TransparencyLog, buildCheckpointNote, signTreeHead, verifyTreeHeadSignature, verifyTransparencyAppend, getTransparencyLog, _resetTransparencyLogsForTests, TRANSPARENCY_LOG_SPEC. merkle.js#148 exports verifyInclusion but transparency-log.js does NOT re-export it. Users must 'import { verifyInclusion } from ./merkle.js' directly or call verifyTransparencyAppend with a full audit structure.
gaps.fix: Add to transparency-log.js exports: `export { verifyInclusion } from './merkle.js';` so users can call `import { verifyInclusion } from 'src/transparency-log.js'` and verify a proof: `const ok = verifyInclusion({ leafHash, leafIndex, treeSize, inclusionPath, root });`
gaps.effort: Trivial (1 re-export line)

gaps.issue: MEDIUM: keyFingerprint() returns 128-bit (first 32 hex chars of SHA256) identifier for Ed25519 keys. Industry standard for collision-resistant key IDs is 160+ bits. At scale (>2^64 auditors), birthday attacks become feasible. Current design is pragmatic for small-scale auditor networks but will collide if kolm onboards hundreds of thousands of independent auditors.
gaps.evidence: src/ed25519.js:102-110, line 109: 'return hash.slice(0, 32)' returns 128 bits (32 hex = 16 bytes = 128 bits). No test asserting minimum bit length or collision risk.
gaps.fix: Use full 256-bit SHA256: `return hash;` (return all 64 hex chars). OR: add configuration: `return hash.slice(0, Math.max(64, process.env.KOLM_FINGERPRINT_BITS || 32));` and document: 'In production, set KOLM_FINGERPRINT_BITS=64 (256-bit fingerprints) to support 1M+ auditors.' Change verifier comment from 'hex32' to 'hex64' and update tests.
gaps.effort: Low (1 line change + docs, but requires migration logic for existing fingerprints)

gaps.issue: MEDIUM: Binder verification requires RECIPE_RECEIPT_SECRET to verify HMAC chain integrity—offline auditors without the secret can verify Ed25519 signatures (Wave 149) and Sigstore bundles (Wave 150), but cannot independently verify the 5-step HMAC audit chain. Documented as 'unverified' state, but it limits third-party audit independence.
gaps.evidence: src/binder.js:404-458; line 422 'HMAC unverified — RECIPE_RECEIPT_SECRET not present in this environment'; no independent HMAC verification possible. Wave 149 Ed25519 + Wave 150 Sigstore mitigate this for most use cases, but HMAC chain itself remains secret-gated.
gaps.fix: Design choice already documented: Ed25519 + Sigstore are the public-verifiable layers; HMAC is a secondary integrity check for tenants holding the secret. If third-party auditors need full chain verification, the tenant must share RECIPE_RECEIPT_SECRET in a secure out-of-band channel (not recommended for production). Alternatively: publish the HMAC secret in a transparency log + checkpoint signature so auditors can re-verify the chain with a signed reference. Current design is acceptable if documented clearly on /security page.
gaps.effort: Medium (if re-architecture needed) or Low (if docs clarification suffices)

gaps.issue: MEDIUM: crossCheckAttestation() allows k_score.composite drift up to 1e-4 (0.0001, line 367). In regulatory contexts where audit scores are displayed to decision-makers, two auditors observing score 0.8500 vs. 0.8501 (drift=0.0001) pass the check silently but may represent rounding differences in the same direction (e.g., one auditor rounds to 4 decimals, another to 5), creating hidden discrepancies in audit-trail scoring.
gaps.evidence: src/auditor-attestation.js:365-373, line 367: 'if (drift > 1e-4)' — allows ±0.00009 undetected.
gaps.fix: Document the tolerance explicitly in code comments: '// 1e-4 tolerance accounts for IEEE 754 rounding across independent auditor observations; stricter verification may require exact match. Regulatory deployments should validate rounding strategy.' OR: make tolerance configurable: `const ALLOWED_DRIFT = process.env.KOLM_AUDITOR_DRIFT_TOLERANCE || 1e-4;` Add test: assert that drift=1e-5 passes, drift=1.5e-4 fails.
gaps.effort: Low (docs + optional env var)

gaps.issue: MEDIUM: CI/CD timing weakness—kolm-ci-pipeline.yml line 38 checks `if: ${{ secrets.KOLM_KEY != '' }}` at job START, not at GitHub Actions registration time. If KOLM_KEY is rotated or revoked mid-pipeline (e.g., after compile but before publish), a long-running job may continue using the old/revoked key without notification.
gaps.evidence: .github/workflows/kolm-ci-pipeline.yml:38 'if: ${{ secrets.KOLM_KEY != '' }}'—evaluates once at job start, not re-checked in publish step.
gaps.fix: Add key freshness check in publish step: before calling kolm publish, re-read the secret and compare SHA256 to the version used at compile time; fail if mismatched. OR: add expiry logic to KOLM_KEY (e.g., timestamp-based rotation), and fail at publish if key age > threshold. Document: 'Keys rotated during a multi-hour job will not be detected; plan rotation windows outside CI runs.'
gaps.effort: Medium (requires secret version tracking + publish-step validation)

gaps.issue: MEDIUM: No explicit HSM/signing-service integration path—builder + auditor keys are assumed to be PEM files or env vars. Enterprise deployments (AWS Signing, Azure Key Vault, GCP KMS, Yubikey, TPM) have no first-class support. Customers will fork to add HSM signing, creating unmaintained divergent implementations that verifiers cannot audit.
gaps.evidence: src/ed25519.js#loadOrCreateDefaultSigner, src/auditor-attestation.js#loadAuditorKeyFromEnv — only file/env var loaders. No interface for hardware security modules.
gaps.fix: Define a SignerInterface: `{ sign(data: Buffer): Promise<Buffer>, publicKey(): Promise<PEM> }`. Implement in-tree: FileSystemSigner, EnvVarSigner, AwsKmsSigner (stub with docs). Refactor sign() calls to use the interface. Document: 'For HSM integration, implement SignerInterface and inject via KOLM_SIGNER_IMPL=aws-kms or similar.' This is a roadmap item; for now, document the expected interface so customers can implement without diverging.
gaps.effort: High (interface design + at least 1 cloud integration stub)

gaps.issue: MEDIUM: INTOTO_RECEIPT_VERSION hardcoded as 'w921-intoto-receipt-v1' with no version negotiation or backward-compatibility layer. If a future release changes the statement shape or adds optional fields, old verifiers may silently reject new receipts or miss fields.
gaps.evidence: src/intoto-receipt.js:56 'export const INTOTO_RECEIPT_VERSION = 'w921-intoto-receipt-v1''; no version negotiation in signInTotoBundle or verifyInTotoBundle.
gaps.fix: Add version field to bundle.verificationMaterial: `version: INTOTO_RECEIPT_VERSION`. In verifyInTotoBundle, log version but do NOT fail if version differs (soft-warn). Maintain a MAX_SUPPORTED_VERSION array and fail if version > max. Add migration docs: 'v2 receipts are backward-compatible; verifiers will warn on v1 but accept.'
gaps.effort: Low (add version field + soft-fail logic)

gaps.issue: MEDIUM: Cross-tenant leak risk in transparency log—_rows() filters by tenant_id, but store.find() result order is unspecified. If two concurrent requests call TransparencyLog with different tenant_ids on a low-isolation store, rows may blur. No explicit concurrency test in wave-suite (tests run with --test-concurrency=1).
gaps.evidence: src/transparency-log.js:113-123, lines 115-116 — find() result is sorted but assumes store returns ALL rows (not tenant-filtered at store level). If store implementation is multi-tenant-aware but find() predicates are applied client-side, concurrent threads could observe out-of-order rows.
gaps.fix: Add test: spin up two TransparencyLog instances with different tenant_ids, append concurrently, verify row isolation. Ensure store.find() returns sorted results. Document: 'TransparencyLog assumes single-threaded access per tenant; for multi-threaded deployments, use a store with per-tenant locking or explicit row ordering guarantees.'
gaps.effort: Low (test + minor docs)

gaps.issue: MEDIUM: No public specification document for kolm's signing/attestation model—modules have excellent inline comments, but no RFC/formal spec document published. Enterprise auditors often require formal specifications (RFC, IEEE 802 style) to approve integration. Code comments alone are insufficient for regulatory review.
gaps.evidence: src/ed25519.js, auditor-attestation.js, intoto-receipt.js, transparency-log.js all have thorough header comments, but no external-facing RFC or spec document exists. README.md links to public/docs/rs-1.md (artifact runtime spec) but not a cryptographic spec.
gaps.fix: Publish a formal spec: RFC-style document (RFC XXXX: kolm Attestation and Signing) covering: (1) Ed25519 signing scheme (RFC 8037 + kolm extensions), (2) N+7 three-party attestation model, (3) DSSE envelope (RFC 9453), (4) Merkle transparency log (RFC 6962/9162), (5) checkpoint notes (C2SP), (6) conformance levels (not a compute proof, only key-custody attestation). Host at /docs/rfc-attestation.md or similar. Version it. This is a high-effort roadmap item but essential for enterprise trust.
gaps.effort: High (40-page formal spec + review cycles)

gaps.issue: LOW: No explicit end-to-end test for receipt signature rotation—rotateTenantReceiptSecret() preserves old keys in previous_receipt_secrets[], but no test verifies that artifacts signed with an old key still verify after key rotation, or that verifiers correctly walk the fallback ring.
gaps.evidence: src/auth.js:288-310 implements rotation and fallback ring logic. tests/ has no test_artifact_signed_old_key.test.js or equivalent verifying old-signature verification post-rotation.
gaps.fix: Add test: create artifact with key_v1, rotate to key_v2, verify the artifact still verifies against the fallback ring including key_v1. Prune key_v1, re-verify and assert it fails.
gaps.effort: Low (1 test file, <50 lines)

code_audit.summary: The src/ directory (560 JS files, 9.8MB, ~8,600 LOC in sample) exhibits solid separation of concerns with excellent cryptographic core modules (ed25519.js, auditor-attestation.js, intoto-receipt.js, transparency-log.js) that follow RFC standards rigorously. However, architectural sprawl across 20+ subdirectories, heavy router.js centralization (27K lines), and 96+ modules importing from capture-store/router create tight coupling. The attestation stack is state-of-the-art but isolated from the product domain; the overall coherence suffers from missing module boundaries and naming collisions (36 capture-*, 7 routers, multiple store variants). Test coverage is comprehensive (730 tests, wave-named), but no thread-safety guarantees exist for concurrent test execution despite package.json setting test-concurrency=1. Key-loading functions lack explicit Ed25519 type validation, secrets-vault.js fails silently on Windows chmod, and transparency-log lacks external ledger integration. For "world-class auditor" credibility, the architecture should refactor src/ into feature-bounded subdomains with explicit facade layers.
strengths: Cryptographic core (ed25519.js, auditor-attestation.js, intoto-slsa.js, transparency-log.js) implements RFC 8037 (Ed25519), RFC 6962 (Merkle), RFC 9453 (DSSE PAE), and in-toto Statement v1 with exemplary rigor and no external crypto dependencies—reviewed inline comments demonstrate deep RFC literacy and threat-model awareness.

Auditor attestation architecture (Wave 166) enforces third-party key isolation via distinct KOLM_AUDITOR_ED25519_* env vars and binder check #22 rejects self-attestation (auditor!=builder fingerprint cross-check), preventing a single party from signing both layers—this is architecturally sound.

Signing-key lifecycle (loadOrCreateDefaultSigner, Wave 149) provides reasonable defaults (per-machine ~/.kolm/signing-key.pem mode 0o600) with fallback to explicit env-var production path; KOLM_ED25519_DISABLE flag allows opt-out; layered HMAC+Ed25519 (HMAC inside, Ed25519 outside) preserves backward compatibility.

Attestation validation is comprehensive: validateAuditorAttestationBlock checks spec/alg/required-fields, recomputes fingerprint from public key (prevents substitution), verifies Ed25519 signature over canonical JSON, and validates post-signature block hash—zero shortcuts taken.

Transparency-log RFC 6962 Merkle tree implementation (leafHash, computeRoot, inclusionPath, verifyInclusion) is correct, deterministic, and self-contained (no @sigstore deps); hash chain (prev_hash linking) adds tamper-evidence layer on top of Merkle.

Test suite (730 tests, wave-named) includes proper isolation: wave166 pins cwd, isolates env vars per test, creates temp dirs with random suffixes, uses deterministic fake secrets—demonstrates maturity in test hygiene.

Modular driver selection (store.js + capture-store.js) supports json/sqlite/vercel-postgres/vercel-kv with graceful degradation; durable write path (writeFileDurably) uses atomic rename + fsync + cross-platform file-lock retry.

Key fingerprint derivation is stable (SHA256 of SPKI DER, not PEM string) and exposed publicly (ed25519.publicKeyJwk exports as RFC 8037 OKP JWK for .well-known/jwks.json)—correct for third-party verification.
Canonical JSON is implemented consistently (auditor-attestation.js, intoto-slsa.js, transparency-log.js use same canonicalJson pattern with sorted keys) to support deterministic hashing.

Documentation quality within core crypto modules is exceptional: every function has header comments naming RFCs, explaining invariants, listing env vars, and calling out threat boundaries (e.g., 'Wave 0 HMAC is symmetric, cannot prove to third party').

gaps.issue: No explicit Ed25519 key type validation in loadAuditorKeyFromEnv() and loadAuditorKeyFromFile()
gaps.evidence: src/auditor-attestation.js:111-133, src/auditor-attestation.js:138-159: Both functions call crypto.createPrivateKey(pem) but never check keyObj.asymmetricKeyType === 'ed25519'. An RSA, ECDSA, or mismatched key will not throw an error at load time; it will only fail when ed25519Sign() is called, and sign() may silently accept a non-Ed25519 key (Node.js crypto.sign(null, buf, keyObj) infers the algorithm from keyObj type). This allows a malformed auditor attestation block with the wrong key type embedded.
gaps.fix: After crypto.createPrivateKey(pem), assert keyObj.asymmetricKeyType === 'ed25519' or throw an explicit error: 'auditor key must be Ed25519, got ' + keyObj.asymmetricKeyType. Same for ed25519.js loadSignerKeyFromEnv() and loadOrCreateDefaultSigner().
gaps.effort: < 5 mins per function (3 locations, 1 line each)

gaps.issue: secrets-vault.js chmod(0o600) wrapped in silent try-catch; fails silently on Windows, potentially exposing API keys
gaps.evidence: src/secrets-vault.js:49 and :71: 'try { fs.chmodSync(p, 0o600); } catch {} // deliberate: cleanup' — Windows does not support chmod; file is created world-readable by default. If an attacker has local access to the dev machine, ~/.kolm/secrets-vault.json and ~/.kolm/secrets-vault.key are readable by other users. Comment says 'deliberate' but no validation checks that chmod actually succeeded; callers have no way to know the file is unreadable.
gaps.fix: (a) On Windows, use NTFS ACL apis (icacls via child_process) or explicitly check process.platform and error-fail on Windows for now, or (b) verify the file's actual permissions after chmod via fs.statSync().mode & 0o077 === 0 and throw if chmod failed. Document the Windows limitation clearly.
gaps.effort: 15-30 mins (research Windows ACL approach or add explicit permission validation)

gaps.issue: keyFingerprint() uses only 32 hex chars (128 bits) of SHA256 output; susceptible to birthday attacks at scale
gaps.evidence: src/ed25519.js:102-109: 'return hash.slice(0, 32)' returns 32 hex chars = 128 bits. For N independent auditors, birthday collision probability ~= N^2 / 2^128. At 2^32 auditors (~4B), collision risk becomes measurable. Industry standard (X.509 fingerprints, TLS cert pins) uses 160+ bits.
gaps.fix: Increase to 48-64 hex chars (192-256 bits) to match SHA256 output length or use the full 64 hex chars. This is a breaking change for existing deployments; add a version field (fingerprint_algo: 'sha256-v2-hex64') to make future rotation explicit.
gaps.effort: 1-2 hours (schema migration + version bump + test updates)

gaps.issue: Transparency log (src/transparency-log.js) has no external ledger anchor; Merkle root is in-process memory or single-machine file
gaps.evidence: src/transparency-log.js:103-122: TransparencyLog constructor accepts store (kolm's src/store.js) but emits no C2SP signed-note checkpoint to a distributed ledger (Rekor, CT logs, etc.). appendEvent() and checkpoint() store rows locally but have no hook to publish checkpoints upstream. Claim: 'tamper-evident' but a malicious operator can mutate old rows on disk and regenerate the Merkle root offline; a verifier with only the local log cannot detect this (needs a signed checkpoint from a public witness).
gaps.fix: Add optional checkpoint publishing to a remote endpoint (Rekor-like webhook or file store). Emit a signed checkpoint (origin, tree_size, root_hash, timestamp, signature_ed25519) and persist the signed note. Add a verifyCheckpointSignature() export so verifiers can validate the signed note without querying the log. Make this optional (no-op if endpoint not configured) so local testing still works.
gaps.effort: 4-6 hours (add checkpoint builder + signer + HTTP publish + offline verifier + tests)

gaps.issue: No verifyInclusionProof() export; users cannot independently verify Merkle inclusion without calling internal verifyTransparencyAppend()
gaps.evidence: src/transparency-log.js:130-180 implements inclusionPath() and returns a proof, but there is no public verifyInclusionProof(proof, leaf_hash, root_hash) function. Callers can read the proof from the log but cannot verify it offline in an air-gapped environment or in a third-party tool.
gaps.fix: Export verifyInclusionProof(proof, leaf_hash, root_hash) that recomputes the Merkle path and confirms root_hash. Make it pure (no store calls, no side effects). Add unit tests with known Merkle proofs.
gaps.effort: 30-45 mins (write verifyInclusionProof + 3-5 tests)

gaps.issue: Auditor attestation cross-check (crossCheckAttestation) allows k_score.composite drift up to 1e-4 (0.0001); aggressive for regulatory audit contexts
gaps.evidence: src/auditor-attestation.js (reading binder.js check #22 logic): The check allows eval_score and k_score.composite to drift if difference <= 1e-4. If auditor rounds 0.74999 → 0.75 but builder rounds 0.75001 → 0.75, both pass the 1e-4 gate but may have re-signed different model behaviors. For SOC 2 or FDA audit trails, this level of tolerance is not documented and could fail external audit.
gaps.fix: Document the tolerance explicitly in auditor-attestation.js as a constant (CROSS_CHECK_TOLERANCE_K_SCORE = 1e-4) with a comment explaining why this value was chosen. Add a strict mode (crossCheckAttestation(block, manifest, {strict: true})) that requires exact match. Emit a warning in logs when tolerance is invoked.
gaps.effort: 30 mins (add constant + comment + strict-mode flag + warning log)

gaps.issue: loadAuditorKeyFromEnv() and loadAuditorKeyFromFile() do not validate that the key is NOT the same as the builder key at load time
gaps.evidence: src/auditor-attestation.js:111-133, :138-159: Both functions return {privateKey, publicKey, key_fingerprint} without checking if this key is already used by the builder (KOLM_ED25519_PRIVATE_KEY or ~/.kolm/signing-key.pem). If an operator accidentally reuses the same key for both auditor and builder, the check #22 'auditor != builder' in binder.js will catch it at verify time, but build time should reject this loudly.
gaps.fix: In loadAuditorKeyFromEnv(), after loading, compare fingerprint to the builder's key. Load builder key via loadOrCreateDefaultSigner() from ed25519.js and raise an error if fingerprints match. Document this check in the function comment.
gaps.effort: 30-45 mins (add builder key load + fingerprint comparison + error message + test)

gaps.issue: src/router.js is 27K lines; violates single-responsibility principle; contains all three layers (capture, compile, audit) and admin routes
gaps.evidence: src/router.js:1-100 imports from 80+ modules (synthesis, build-preview, kscore, assistant, auth, email, verifier, library, store, metrics, recall, stripe, capture, teams, provider-vault, groups, tunnel, byoc, deployment-plans, scim, saml-acs, spend-caps, webhooks, model-entitlements, compile-eval-gate, model-update-channel, inference-bench, billing-activation, connectors, lifecycle, evidence-dag, and many more). Line 26998 total. No clear route grouping; a single file defines /v1/capture, /v1/compile, /v1/audit, /admin, /oauth, /scim, webhooks, MCP routes. A developer changing the audit attestation flow must understand all three layers in a single file.
gaps.fix: Refactor router.js into domain-scoped routers: router-capture.js (capture + event-store + quarantine), router-compile.js (synthesis + compile + lifecycle), router-audit.js (auditor attestation + transparency-log + verification), router-admin.js, router-oauth.js, etc. Each exports a (req, res, next) middleware or a route array. Compose them in server.js or a main router. Keep router.js as a lightweight dispatcher.
gaps.effort: 8-12 hours (split into 6-8 routers + test each + smoke-test composition)

gaps.issue: store.js and capture-store.js have overlapping abstractions; no clear separation between persistent-row storage and domain-specific capture logic
gaps.evidence: src/store.js (line 1) is a generic key-value/table facade (insert, update, find, findOne, findByField, findByTenant) supporting json/sqlite drivers. src/capture-store.js (line 1-30) wraps store.js and adds capture-specific logic (observationToCanonicalEvent, insertCapture with copyright flag + heuristic detector, listCaptures, countCaptures, isDurable, health). Neither has a clear interface boundary; 96 modules import from both. When the audit team needs to add attestation records, do they use store.js or create an attestation-store.js?
gaps.fix: Define a Store interface: {insert(table, row), update(table, pred, patch), find(table, pred), all(table), withTransaction(fn), reset()}. Move capture-specific concerns (observationToCanonicalEvent, copyright flagging) into a CaptureService wrapping Store. Create similar AuditService(Store) and VerifyService(Store). Expose a single 'services' module: export {Store, CaptureService, AuditService, VerifyService} so callers depend on the service, not the raw store.
gaps.effort: 6-8 hours (define interfaces, wrap store in 3 service classes, update 96+ imports, test each service)

gaps.issue: Transparency-log tenancy (tenant_id in rows) is not explicitly tested for cross-tenant leakage; _rows() filters but has no isolation gate if store.find() is called with wrong context
gaps.evidence: src/transparency-log.js:113-122: _rows() filters by (r.tenant_id === this.tenant_id) but if store.find() returns rows in arbitrary order or concurrent calls reuse a TransparencyLog instance, tenant boundary could blur. No test in tests/wave166-auditor-attestation.test.js or wave921-govern-receipts-compliance.test.js explicitly validates that appending to tenant_A's log does not return rows in tenant_B's log. The comment W411 in store.js references tenant isolation but transparency-log has no analog gate.
gaps.fix: Add an explicit test: create two TransparencyLog(store, {tenant_id: 'A'}) and {tenant_id: 'B'}), append to A, append to B, verify A.size() == 1, B.size() == 1. Add a tenancy assertion in _rows(): if (this.tenant_id) throw if any returned row has a different tenant_id. Make this a loud fail, not a filter.
gaps.effort: 45 mins (add 2-3 unit tests + add assertion in _rows())

gaps.issue: No named, public integration test validating Ed25519 signature verification works with external verifier (e.g., openssl, Node.js standalone crypto without kolm)
gaps.evidence: tests/wave149-ed25519-default.test.js includes 'ed25519.sign / verify round-trip' but does not export a signed artifact and verify it with an external tool (e.g., openssl ed25519 verify, or a standalone Node.js script that imports only node:crypto and ed25519.js). This means an external auditor who wants to verify a kolm signature offline cannot validate the implementation without trusting kolm's own verify() function.
gaps.fix: Add a test that (a) signs a test payload with ed25519.sign(), (b) exports the public key as PEM, (c) runs 'openssl pkey -inform PEM -text' to confirm it's Ed25519 (on non-Windows CI), (d) calls `openssl pkeyutl -verify ... -in <(echo -n payload | base64) -pubin -inkey pubkey.pem` or equivalent to verify independently. Document the manual command in the test comment so auditors can replicate offline.
gaps.effort: 1.5-2 hours (add openssl verification in test + document manual step + cross-platform path handling)

gaps.issue: CI/CD guard (kolm-ci-pipeline.yml) uses 'if: ${{ secrets.KOLM_KEY != '' }}' at job START, not at action registration; long-running jobs may use revoked keys
gaps.evidence: From the context briefing red-flags section: kolm-ci-pipeline.yml gate evaluates at job START. If KOLM_KEY is rotated mid-pipeline (e.g., after a security incident), a long-running job (hours) may start with the old key, finish with stale credentials, and publish artifacts signed by a revoked key. A downstream verifier has no way to know the key was rotated.
gaps.fix: (a) Re-evaluate the secret at each action step using a nested 'if' or (b) fetch the current secret at build time and abort if it differs from what was stored at job start, (c) add a check in intoto-receipt.js or binder.js that logs a warning if the signing key's creation timestamp is more than N days old (e.g., 'key is 30 days old, consider rotation'). Document the limitation in CI/CD docs.
gaps.effort: 1-2 hours (add re-check logic in actions + signing-time warning in binder)

gaps.issue: INTOTO_RECEIPT_VERSION hardcoded as 'w921-intoto-receipt-v1' with no version negotiation or backward-compatibility layer
gaps.evidence: src/intoto-receipt.js:56: 'export const INTOTO_RECEIPT_VERSION = w921-intoto-receipt-v1'. If a future release adds optional fields or changes the statement shape, old verifiers will either reject new receipts (if they do strict schema validation) or silently miss new fields. No version-check in buildInferencePredicate() or receiptSubjects().
gaps.fix: Add a buildReceiptWithVersion(receipt, version = INTOTO_RECEIPT_VERSION) function that callers explicitly pass. Bump to v2 if breaking changes are needed. Add a unit test that verifies a v1 receipt still validates in verifyDsseEnvelope() (even if v2 is current).
gaps.effort: 1 hour (add version parameter + test + documentation)

gaps.issue: pae() function does not assert exact byte-length; a single character-vs-byte confusion would break cosign interop silently
gaps.evidence: src/intoto-slsa.js:62-74: pae() builds the DSSE PAE correctly, but tests do not assert exact PAE byte output against the RFC 9453 example. Comments cite 'DSSEv1 29 http://example.com/HelloWorld 11 hello world' but test suite (wave921-intoto-attestation.test.js or wave921-govern-crypto.test.js) does not hard-assert the exact bytes produced. If a future refactor accidentally uses string.length instead of buffer.length, the signature would verify in kolm but fail in cosign.
gaps.fix: Add a unit test: assert that pae('http://example.com/HelloWorld', 'hello world').toString('hex') === exact-expected-hex-bytes. Hardcode the expected bytes from RFC 9453 example.
gaps.effort: 30 mins (add 1 test with hardcoded expected bytes)

gaps.issue: Module sprawl: 560 JS files across 20+ subdirectories with no clear architectural layers or bounded contexts
gaps.evidence: src/ contains: cloud-providers/, cloud/, compute/, data/, device-adapters/, distill-runners/, distill/, importers/, migrations/, providers/, runners/, services/, storage/, store-drivers/, teachers/, PLUS 520 files at root level. 36 files match 'capture-*', 7 files match '*router*', 15+ files match 'store*' or '*-store*'. No clear domain boundaries; module naming does not signal layer (e.g., is 'synthesis.js' compile-phase or runtime?). A new team member looking for 'where does audit logging go?' would find src/audit.js, src/audit-log-routes.js, src/audit-trail.js, src/evidence-store.js, src/evidence-dag.js, and multiple wave-*.js files touching this space.
gaps.fix: Organize src/ into feature-aligned domains: src/capture/ (capture.js, capture-store.js, capture-*.js, event-store.js, event-schema.js), src/compile/ (synthesis.js, compile.js, compile-eval-gate.js, build-preview.js, compile-targets.js), src/audit/ (auditor-attestation.js, intoto-receipt.js, intoto-slsa.js, binder.js, evidence-dag.js, evidence-store.js, transparency-log.js, merkle.js), src/infra/ (store.js, store-drivers/*, cache.js, lake.js, secrets-vault.js). Expose facade modules: import {CaptureService} from './capture/index.js'; import {CompileService} from './compile/index.js'; import {AuditService} from './audit/index.js'. Maintain backward compatibility via index.js re-exports for 2-3 major versions.
gaps.effort: 20-30 hours (move 560 files into 4-6 directories, update all imports, write facade layers, test, update docs)

gaps.issue: Test concurrency safety: package.json uses --test-concurrency=1 but tests share KOLM_ED25519_KEY_STORE env var across runs; parallel test execution would collide on ~/.kolm/signing-key.pem
gaps.evidence: src/ed25519.js:174-232 generates per-machine key at ~/.kolm/signing-key.pem. tests/wave166-auditor-attestation.test.js:72-95 isolates env vars per test but if test-concurrency is set >1 (e.g., 'npm run test:parallel'), tests will race on the same ~/.kolm location. One test generates a fresh key, another reads it, and builder fingerprints will collide, breaking the 'auditor != builder' check.
gaps.fix: In every test that uses loadOrCreateDefaultSigner(), set KOLM_ED25519_KEY_STORE to a unique temp directory: tests call it 'isolateEnv(t)' but KOLM_ED25519_KEY_STORE is not isolated. gaps.issue: SECRETS VAULT CHMOD FAILS SILENTLY ON WINDOWS: src/secrets-vault.js#getOrCreateKey() and #writeVault() wrap fs.chmodSync() in try/catch with 'deliberate: cleanup' comment. On Windows or restricted filesystems, chmod fails silently and the vault file may remain world-readable. No explicit check that chmod actually succeeded or that file is now unreadable by others. If a developer or audit finds the vault is group/world-readable, the failure is undiagnosed.
gaps.evidence: src/secrets-vault.js:49 'try { fs.chmodSync(p, 0o600); } catch {} // deliberate: cleanup' and line 71 identical pattern. No assertion or warning when chmod fails. Platform-specific: Windows NTFS does not support Unix permissions; chmod is a no-op.
gaps.fix: (1) Replace silent catch with conditional logic: check platform (process.platform !== 'win32') and only chmod on Unix. (2) On Windows, document that vault file is created in user's home dir (usually ACL-protected by OS). (3) Add explicit test in wave149-ed25519-default.test.js or new test: verify that signing-key.pem has restricted permissions or issue a warning if running on an unsupported platform. (4) Add comment clarifying: 'chmod is best-effort; Windows permissions are managed by NTFS ACLs, not Unix mode bits.'
gaps.effort: Low (50 lines, 45 min including test)

gaps.issue: CI/CD SIGNING GUARD TIMING WINDOW: kolm-ci-pipeline.yml line 38 uses 'if: ${{ secrets.KOLM_KEY != '' }}' which evaluates at GitHub Actions workflow parsing time, not at job start. If KOLM_KEY is rotated mid-pipeline (e.g., operator revokes old key), a long-running job (e.g., compile phase) may still have loaded the old key in memory and will use it to sign the artifact. The revocation is not noticed because the key was already retrieved. No per-step key validation.
gaps.evidence: .github/workflows/kolm-ci-pipeline.yml:38 'if: ${{ secrets.KOLM_KEY != '' }}'. This guard is static; it does not re-validate the key after compile or test phases. If compile takes 10 minutes and KOLM_KEY is rotated in minute 5, artifact will be signed with revoked key.
gaps.fix: (1) Add a 'Validate signing key freshness' step before the Publish step that fetches the key and verifies it matches the Git commit message or a pinned SHA (GitHub Actions does not support dynamic secret rotation in-pipeline, but you can add a check that fails if the key appears in a revocation list). (2) Document the limitation: 'Once a key is loaded into a workflow job, it is held for the duration of the job. Rotate keys between runs, not during runs.' (3) For future: hook to AWS Secrets Manager or HashiCorp Vault for just-in-time key retrieval per signing operation.
gaps.effort: Medium (2-3 hours to add revocation check step and document)

gaps.issue: INTOTO_RECEIPT_VERSION HARDCODED WITH NO FORWARD-COMPATIBILITY: src/intoto-receipt.js exports INTOTO_RECEIPT_VERSION = 'w921-intoto-receipt-v1' but there is no version negotiation or backward-compatibility layer. If a future update changes statement shape (e.g., adds optional fields, restructures predicate), old verifiers may reject new receipts silently or miss new fields without warning. Tests lock in the v1 shape but provide no upgrade path.
gaps.evidence: src/intoto-receipt.js:56 'export const INTOTO_RECEIPT_VERSION = 'w921-intoto-receipt-v1''. Verifier tests (wave921-intoto-attestation.test.js) check for exact shape match but no test for version negotiation or forward-compatibility.
gaps.fix: (1) Add versioning logic to toInTotoStatement(): accept an optional version parameter, default to 'w921-intoto-receipt-v1'. (2) Add a verifyInTotoBundle(bundle, acceptedVersions=[]) parameter so verifiers can opt-in to future versions. (3) Add test case: verifyInTotoBundle rejects a v2 receipt when acceptedVersions=['w921-intoto-receipt-v1']. (4) Document in header: 'Version 1 is locked; future versions must be backported for at least 2 releases to allow SDK updates to propagate.'
gaps.effort: Medium (2-3 hours including tests)

gaps.issue: NO EXPLICIT KEYTYPE VALIDATION IN ED25519 LOADERS EITHER: While ed25519.js does not have the same auditor key issue, loadSignerKeyFromEnv() also does NOT validate that crypto.createPrivateKey(pem) returned an Ed25519 key. site_audit.summary: Design system tokens and CSS architecture are world-class (monochrome brand, responsive typography via clamp, excellent dark/light theming, proper spacing rhythm). However, the homepage hero and overall visual identity are fundamentally misaligned with the product pivot from "AI control plane" to "AI-security audit platform signed by elite researchers." The credibility stack (Ed25519, auditor attestation, Merkle transparency logs) is production-grade in code but invisible on the public site. Hero still says "Own your company's AI layer" (old direction) instead of "Audit your AI. Get signed proof." No prominent researcher/auditor network visibility, no independent receipt-verification demo, no credibility badges. Visual quality is polished but reads premium-SaaS, not premium-security-audit. Would not embarrass on design execution alone, but the positioning mismatch and missing credibility signals would immediately undermine trust with elite auditors.
strengths: Design tokens comprehensively documented with 50+ CSS custom properties organized by category (design-tokens.css). Monochrome brand philosophy clearly stated: 'Ink. No chromatic brand.' Color scale well-calibrated: ink-1 through ink-4 provide proper contrast hierarchy.

Responsive typography system uses CSS clamp() for fluid scaling without media-query proliferation (e.g., h1: clamp(38px, 5.2vw, 72px)). Font-feature-settings enabled for professional appearance (ss01, ss03, cv11). Inter + JetBrains Mono stack properly configured.

Dark/light theming thoughtfully implemented via data-theme attribute toggle. Light mode: cool slate (f3f5f7 bg, #111 ink). Dark mode: warm candlelit (0a0c10 bg, e6e9ee ink) with subtle orange/brown undertones. Proper color inversion ensures readability on both.

Section spacing uses consistent 4/8px scale with generous breathing room (64-144px vertical rhythm via clamp). Hairline dividers (1px) between sections with subtle inset highlight (0 1px 0 rgba(255,255,255,0.55)) create visual rhythm without heaviness.

Micro-interactions executed well: card hover lift +2px, button press feedback (translateY 0.5px), focus rings with outline-offset: 3px (keyboard-only via focus-visible), smooth transitions 120-250ms with spring easing (cubic-bezier 0.2, 0.7, 0.2, 1).

Accessibility solid: focus-visible states site-wide, reduced-motion media query honored (durations collapse to 1ms), semantic HTML in nav (aria-labels, button/link roles), skip-to-content link present (#ksSkip), color contrast >7:1 on dark mode.

Grid systems use modern CSS (minmax(), auto-fit) for responsive layouts. Mobile-first approach: max-width 640px breakpoints applied first, desktop refinements in media queries. No hard breakpoint collisions observed.

Card system coherent: .card base styles (border, shadow, background), .card:hover (lift, box-shadow increase), reduced-motion safety (transition: none). Inherited by multiple specialized cards (.hp-card, .fr-card, .kolm-whatis__surface).
